coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoparticles combined with amino-functionalized UiO-66 for sensitive detection of glutathione
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Panpan
Li, Zhijian
Gong, Congcong
Yao, Ruihong
Fan, Congbin
Chen, Zhao
Pu, Shouzhi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a fluorescent nanosensor has been constructed for detection of glutathione (GSH) based on NH2-UiO-66 and AgNPs. The NH2-UiO-66 was synthesized where 2-amino-terephthalic acid as the organic ligand and Zr4+ as the center metal ions. The AgNPs can enhance the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 based on metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect. Moreover, in the present of GSH, the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was quenched via electrostatic interaction and Ag-S reaction. The present sensing strategy shows good linear relation with the concentration of GSH in the range of 0.2–1.0 μM and 1.0–30 μM, and the limit of detection is 79 nM. Furthermore, our fluorescent nanosensor was utilized to detect GSH in human serum with a recovery of 96.8–102.5%. The results indicated that NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs is successfully applied for high sensitive and selective detection of GSH in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH2-UiO-66
AgNPs
Glutathione
Metal enhanced fluorescence
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 41867053 ), the Science and Technology Research Project of Education Department of Jiangxi Province ( GJJ190615 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province ( 20202BAB213018 ).

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41867053), the Science and Technology Research Project of Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ190615), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20202BAB213018). The human serum samples were collected at the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University. All the experiments in this work were carried out in compliance with ethical standards, and conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki and approved by the Ethics Committee of Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A) NH2-UiO-66 and (B) NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs, and TEM image (C) NH2-UiO-66 and (D) NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs.
XPS full spectra of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs. XPS fine spectra of (B) Ag 3d, (C)C1 s, (D) O 1 s, (E) N 1 s, and (F) Zr 3d.
(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of NH2-UiO-66, AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NH2-UiO-66, NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs + 30 μM GSH (λex = 330 nm).
Time-resolved fluorescence decays of NH2-UiO-66 and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs (A) and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs + GSH (B).

(A) The fluorescence intensity of the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs at different GSH concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 μM respectively). (B) Standard curve of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs for detection of GSH.
Selectivity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs for detection of GSH for comparing with various amino acids.
Schematic illustration of preparing NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs and the principle of fluorescent detection of GSH.
Comparison of LOD of GSH between AgNPs@NH2-UiO-66 and other fluorescence probes.
Results for the determination of GSH in serum samples

head.ce:title: Silver nanoparticles combined with amino-functionalized UiO-66 for sensitive detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: YuZhang Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: YuZhang Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China (S. Pu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The NH2-UiO-66 was synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method where 2-amino-terephthalic acid as the organic ligand and Zr4+ as the center metal ions. The AgNPs can enhance the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 based on metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect. Moreover, in the present of GSH, the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was quenched via electrostatic interaction and Ag-S reaction. The method was successfully applied to the determination of GSH in human serum.

Herein, a fluorescent nanosensor has been constructed for detection of glutathione (GSH) based on NH2-UiO-66 and AgNPs. The NH2-UiO-66 was synthesized where 2-amino-terephthalic acid as the organic ligand and Zr4+ as the center metal ions. The AgNPs can enhance the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 based on metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect. Moreover, in the present of GSH, the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was quenched via electrostatic interaction and Ag-S reaction. The present sensing strategy shows good linear relation with the concentration of GSH in the range of 0.2–1.0 μM and 1.0–30 μM, and the limit of detection is 79 nM. Furthermore, our fluorescent nanosensor was utilized to detect GSH in human serum with a recovery of 96.8–102.5%. The results indicated that NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs is successfully applied for high sensitive and selective detection of GSH in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a significant nonprotein thiol, is a tripeptide which consist of glutamic acid, cysteine and glycine [1]. It plays an extremely important role in numerous physiological and pathological processes [2]. Abnormal levels of GSH will cause serious diseases, such as cancer, Alzheimer’s disease, diabetes and acquired immune deficiency syndrome [2-4]. Therefore, accurately and rapidly to detect GSH in biological has become one of the most important assignments. At present, the analytical techniques for detection of GSH mainly include electrochemical [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6], mass spectroscopy [7], and fluorescence spectroscopy [8]. Among them, the fluorescent approach has received a growing number of attentions because of the advantages of good sensitivity, high selectivity, and fast response [9]. Amino-functionalized zirconium-based MOFs (NH2-UiO-66) demonstrate excellent stability, low cost, and environmentally friendly [10,11]. However, NH2-UiO-66 shows weak fluorescence which obstructs its direct application in fluorescence detection. Up to now, a number of NH2-UiO-66-based nanoprobes have been developed by post-synthetic modification. Although excellent fluorescence intensity has been achieved by post-synthetic modification, they often suffer from the drawbacks of complicated multistep synthesis and harsh reaction, which may cause the collapse of the frameworks [12,13]. Hence, it is of the significant importance to effectively enhance the emission intensity of NH2-UiO-66.

The fluorescence is enhanced when the fluorescence probe is located near the metallic particles [14]. This phenomenon, called metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) also known as plasma-enhanced fluorescence (PEF) or surface-enhanced fluorescence (SEF) [15], originates from surface plasmon coupling between the metal nanoparticles and fluorophores [16]. The emission intensity of fluorophores close to metal nanoparticles (NPs) could be drastically increased by near-field, which changes the radiation attenuation rate and enhances coupling efficiency of the emission to the far-field [17]. Early reports indicated that metal nanoparticles could be significantly enhancing the fluorescence emission of nearby fluorophores. MEF has been an effective alternative strategy to expand fluorescence signal [18], which demonstrated significant potential for diverse applications in chemical/biochemical sensing, diagnostics, and biotechnology [16,19,20].

In the present study, we have designed a fluorescent nanosensor based on NH2-UiO-66 and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for detection of GSH (Scheme 1 ). In this sensing platform, AgNPs can enhance the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 based on MEF effect. It has been reported that GSH can react with AgNPs based on Ag-S and electrostatic interaction. Therefore, in the present of GSH, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs can be quenched due to the interaction between GSH and AgNPs. The fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs declined with increasing GSH concentration (0.2–30 μM) by excitation at 330 nm. Compared to other fluorescence sensor, NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs sensor for GSH detection exhibits a lower detection, simple operation, cost-effective, fast response time, high selectivity and sensitivity. Additionally, the sensor was applied to detection of GSH in human serum and displays a good recovery rate of 97.05–105%.

The NH2-UiO-66 were synthesized according to a previous report [21]. First, 0.37 g ZrCl4 was dissolved in 92 mL DMF. The mixture was stirred for 15 min under a magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, 0.287 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid was added in the above solutions and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 200 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined and heated at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After that, naturally cooling to room temperature, the obtained produce was collected by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min) and washed with ethanol several times. Finally, the yellow powder was dried in vacuum at 60 ℃ for 24 h before further experiments.

AgNPs were prepared as referred to the reported method [22]. Typically, 250 μL of 100 mM AgNO3 and 250 μL of 100 mM trisodium cltrate dihydrate were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water under stirring. Then, under vigorous stirring at room temperature, freshly prepared 6 mL of 25 mM NaBH4 was rapidly added into above solution. The mixed solution was vigorously stirred for 15 min. The change in the color of the sample from colorless to yellow indicated the formation of AgNPs.
NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was synthesized by the following synthetic route. 100 μL of NH2-UiO-66 (0.1 mg/mL) dispersion was added into 50 μL of 0.03 μM AgNPs dispersion and shake well.

150 μL of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs solution was added to 50 μL different concentrations of GSH standard solution and then was diluted with Tris − HCl buffer (20 mM, pH = 7.4) to keep 2 mL. After, the mixed solution was used for fluorescence analysis. To further evaluate the selectivity toward GSH detection, various amino acid were used with 10 times concentration, including histidine (His), Serine (Ser), Alanine (Ala), Lysine (Lys), Leucine (Leu), Isoleucine (Iso), Threonine (Thr), Glutamic acid (Glu-A), Valine (Val), Methionine (Met), Cysteine (DL-Cys), DL-Proline (DL-Pro), Cysteine (Cys), Tyrosine (Tyr), Glycine (Gly), Tryptophan (Try) and Ascorbic acid (AA)

The proposed approach was evaluated the potential application for GSH in serum. The real serum samples were collected in the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University. The real serum samples were treated by centrifugation and different GSH concentrations (0, 0.2, 0.4, 10, 15 and 20 μM) were spiked. The fluorescence spectra of the real samples were recorded. Among them, all the fluorescence measures were repeated at least three times and taking the average was utilized for the further analysis.

The size and morphologies of the nanoparticles were explored through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). By observing the SEM and TEM of NH2-UiO-66 and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs (Fig. 1 ), it can be found that the addition of AgNPs has not change the morphology of NH2-UiO-66. As shown in Fig. 1A and 1C, the NH2-UiO-66 has octahedron structure with a smooth surface and the size of NH2-UiO-66 was about 300–400 nm. After adding AgNPs to NH2-UiO-66 and reacting for 5 min, the smooth surface of NH2-UiO-66 was found to be covered by nanoparticles with a spherical structure (Fig. 1B). To further confirm the modified AgNPs in the NH2 -UiO-66, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) characterizations were performed. Compared the EDS of NH2-UiO-66, the signals of Ag appeared in NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs, which could prove that the hybrid structure was synthesized successfully (Fig. S1A, B).

The crystal structure of NH2-UiO-66 and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs were examined by the X-ray diffraction (PXRD) (Fig. S2A). The diffraction peaks at 7.36, 8.52, and 25.7° are attributed to the peak value of the (111), (200), and (600) planes of the single crystals, respectively, which is consistent with the octahedron structure of NH2-UiO-66 [23,24]. Furthermore, the diffractions peaks of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs materials were in good agreement with the NH2-UiO-66 samples. These observations obviously indicated that the AgNPs hardly affect the crystalline integrity of NH2-UiO-66. We cannot observe the characteristic peak of Ag, which may be the peak intensity of Ag was much weaker in NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs crystal diffraction pattern.

The (Fourier transform infrared) FT-IR spectroscopy was explored to elucidate the structural properties of NH2-UiO-66 and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs (Fig. S2B). For NH2-UiO-66 (curve), the peaks at 3348 cm−1 and 1257 cm−1 were assigned to the N-H stretching vibration of primary amine and C-N stretching vibration. In addition, the peaks located in the range of 1500–1600 cm−1 were attributed to the C = C stretching vibration of benzene ring. After interaction AgNPs, it was found that the N-H peak of NH2-UiO-66 shifted to 3366 cm−1. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the diffraction peaks of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Zr 3p3, Zr 3d5 and Ag 3d were observed in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs, which is in accordance with the results of the EDS of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs. The XPS fine spectra of Ag 3d exhibit two peaks at 369.6 eV and 375.4 eV, which are assigned to the diffraction of Ag 3d1/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 2B) [25,26]. The diffraction peaks at 289.2 eV, 285.7 eV and 284.9 eV in XPS fine spectra of the C 1 s region (Fig. 2C) reveal the existence of O = C-OH, C-N, and C-C/C = C bond [27]. The XPS fine spectra of O 1 s (Fig. 2D) can be divided into two peaks at 532.9 and 532.3 eV, which are corresponding to C = O and Zr-O [28]. The XPS fine spectra of N 1 s can be fitted by two peaks at 400.5 eV and 399.9 eV (Fig. 2E), corresponding to N-H and C-N,[29] which was due to the N atom of the functional group on the organic ligand. The peaks at 189.4 eV and 185.8 eV in high-resolution XPS spectra of the Zr region are respectively related to the diffraction of Zr 3d5 and Zr 3p3 (Fig. 2F).

The thermal stability of NH2-UiO-66 was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. S2C, about 12% weight loss was observed at 30–200 °C owing to the solvent evaporation. It is evident that the weight of NH2-UiO-66 decreased sharply at 319–540 °C, a further weight loss of 30% was ascribed to decomposition of NH2-UiO-66. Besides, the fluorescence stability of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was explored. As shown in Fig. S2D, there is no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs by soaking in Tris − HCl buffer (20 mM, pH = 7.4) for 30 day. The excellent stability of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs paved a way for reliable sensing of GSH.

The NH2-UiO-66 shows a weak fluorescence at 454 nm with the excitation wavelength at 330 nm. After interaction AgNPs, fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66 will enhanced 4.2 times, which demonstrates that AgNPs can enhancement the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 by surfaceplasmonresonance (SPR) (Fig. 3 B). However, upon the introduction of GSH, the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was quenched maybe due to electrostatic interaction and Ag-S reaction between AgNPs with GSH [30-32].

The mechanism of AgNPs enhancement fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66 was investigated. We have performed the zeta potential and UV–visible absorption spectra of AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66. As shown in Fig. S3A, the zeta potential of AgNPs possessed −25.8 mV, while NH2-UiO-66 showed 40.4 mV, indicating that AgNPs interacts with NH2-UiO-66 through electrostatic attraction, leads to decrease the distance between AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66. The fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore is related to the degree of spectral overlap between metal nanoparticles SPR absorption and the maxima emission wavelength of fluorophore [33]. The fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore can reach a maximum when the metal nanoparticles SPR absorption is between the absorption and the emission of fluorophore [14,17,33]. As shown in Fig. S3B, the AgNPs exhibit a SPR absorption band at around 399 nm. The broaden absorption peak at 356 nm is corresponding to NH2-UiO-66. In addition, the maximum emission wavelength of NH2-UiO-66 was at 454 nm (λ ex = 330 nm) (Fig. 3B). The results indicated that the SPR absorption peak of AgNPs is between the maximum emission wavelength and absorption wavelength of NH2-UiO-66, which can attain the excellent fluorescence enhancement [34]. In addition, the absorption peak of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs nanomaterials at 390 nm has a little blue-shifted relative to the AgNPs at 399 nm, which is caused by the binding of AgNPs in NH2-UiO-66. Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetime investigation of NH2-UiO-66 with or without AgNPs was performed to prove that MEF may be due to the enhancement of strong local electric field excitation by AgNPs. After addition of AgNPs, the fluorescence lifetime of the NH2-UiO-66 exhibited a nearly constant lifetime value (Fig. 4 A), indicating the MEF was probably due to AgNPs enhance the excitation of strong local electric field related to local surface plasmon resonance [15,35,36].

To acquire the optimal conditions for GSH detecting, the following factors were explored. According to the confirmed hypothesis of the “MEF”, AgNPs plays an irreplaceable role in the fluorescence enhancement of NH2-UiO-66 and GSH detection. The effect of AgNPs concentration was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66 increased when the AgNPs concentration from 0 to 8.75 nM and no further changes as the concentration of AgNPs increased more than 8.75 nM, the result indicated that the optimal concentration of AgNPs for formation of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs of strongest fluorescence is 8.75 nM. As shown in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66 reached the maximum with 5 min, when addition of AgNPs into NH2-UiO-66. Therefore, 5 min was selected as the incubation time for synthesis of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs.

Fig. S4C evaluated fluorescence emission properties of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs under excitations wavelength from 300 to 370 nm. The fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs gradually enhanced with the increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 330 nm and reached maximal emissions at 330 nm. In order to obtain the best detection performance, 330 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength.

The fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs reduces rapidly and reached equilibrium in 180 s after addition of GSH (Fig. S4D), the result indicated that the fluorescent nanosensors can rapidly detection of GSH. As shown in Fig. S4E, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs has little changes at pH value ranges from 7 to 9. Thence, NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs has displayed excellent stability in the neutral conditions. Under strong acid, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs decreases due to protonation of trisodium cltrate dehydrate as the surface stabling agent and the fluorescence decreased sharply with the pH above 9, owing to deprotonating of trisodium cltrate dehydrate [37]. So that, further experiments were selected pH 8 with the reaction time 3 min for detection of GSH.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was used as fluorescent probes for quantitative analysis of GSH. Fig. 5 A shows the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs with different concentrations of GSH. With the increase of GSH concentration (0–30 μM), the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs decreased gradually. When the concentration of GSH increases from 0.2 μM to 30 μM, two linearity regression equation YF1 = 1.734–0.372 CGSH (0.2 μM ≤ CGSH ≤ 1 μM, RF1 2 = 0.9968) and YF2 = 1.356–0.037 CGSH (1 μM ≤ X ≤ 30 μM, RF2 2 = 0.9977) were obtained (F0 and F mean the fluorescence intensity of NH2-UiO-66 and detection sample, respectively). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 79 nM by using the 3σ IUPAC criteria (3σ/slope) [38]. Compared with previously reported fluorescence probes to detect GSH, this approach exhibits wide detection range, low limitation detection, and rapid response (Table 1 ).

The responses of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs to different amino acid were evaluated including GSH, Hcy, Cys, Ser, Ala, Lys, Leu, Iso, Thr, Glu-A, Val, Met, DL-Cys, DL-Pro, Tyr, Gly, Try and AA (Fig. 6 ). Although the concentration of other amino acid is 10 times higher than GSH, the interfering substances have negligible impact on the fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs. Only Cys and Hcy, the competitive biothiols, have little influence with the detection of GSH. This may be due to the tremendous difference in steric hindrance effects between GSH and others biothiols [44]. The relative molecular mass of GSH (307.32) is higher than that of Cys (121.16) and Hcy (135.18) [44]. The GSH can perfectly encapsulate AgNPs, the MEF effect of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs will be destroyed. For real serum samples, the concentrations of Cys and Hcy are much lower than GSH, so the interference for the GSH detection can be eliminated [45]. The results demonstrated that the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs have high selectivity for detection of GSH.

The potential application of the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was verified with human serum samples using the standard addition method. The spiked different concentrations of GSH for serum samples are 0, 0.2, 0.4, 10, 15 and 20 μM. The concentration of GSH was ranged 2 μM to 12 μM in serum [4]. During the analysis, the serum was diluted 20 times. As shown in Table 2 , the concentration of the blank of GSH was 0.21 μM in serum, within the range of GSH in the healthy human serum. The recoveries ranged from 97.5 to 105%. Compared with other probes for GSH detection, our sensor owns excellent precision and the RSD was lower than 3% (n = 3). These results implied that the proposed GSH detection method based on the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was promising for practical applications.

In order to better understand the sensor mechanism of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs complex for GSH, some characterizations were carried out. The structure of the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs complex and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs + GSH were investigated with XRD characterization. As shown in Fig. S5A, the XRD spectra diffraction peaks of the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs complex remain unchanged, indicating that NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs retained good crystallinity. As shown in Fig. S6, the UV–vis absorption peak of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs shifted from 390 to 394 nm after the adding GSH, which is attributed to the interaction between GSH and AgNPs.

The XPS spectra of the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs before and after GSH were measured. As shown in Fig. S7, after reacting GSH with NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs, there was a shift in the binding energy of Ag from 369.6 eV to 369.8 eV and 375.4 to 375.8 eV. The result indicated that the formation of Ag-S or Ag-N bond between the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs and GSH. The FT-IR spectra of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs before and after GSH were measured. As shown in Fig. S5B, the peak at 2524 cm−1 of sulfydryl (-SH) from GSH disappeared, clearly suggesting that AgNPs may have reacted with GSH to form Ag-S bond[46]. In addition, the zeta potential was used to verify the electrostatic interaction between GSH and AgNPs. As shown in Fig. S3A, the zeta potential of GSH, AgNPs, NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs, and NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs + GSH were + 6.25, −25.8, +29.3, and + 38.6 mV, respectively. The result indicated that the existence of electrostatic attraction between GSH and AgNPs, lead to the MEF effect of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs will be destroyed. Meanwhile, the average fluorescence lifetime values of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs in the presences of GSH did not change (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the lifetime values of carriers unchanged, strongly demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching follows static process instead of dynamic quenching process. The above results indicated that the NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs probes are reacted with GSH via electrostatic interaction or Ag-S bond.

In summary, a novel “turn-off” fluorescent strategy based on AgNPs and NH2-UiO-66 was established to detect GSH. The fluorescence of NH2-UiO-66@AgNPs was quenched by GSH due to electrostatic interaction and Ag-S reaction. Under the best conditions, The strategy exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for detection of GSH. Besides, the approach was used to detect GSH in serum sample, demonstrating its statisfactory applicability in real sample. We believed that this strategy would provides a potential platform for analyzing various biological and clinical samples.

Panpan Huo: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhijian Li: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Congcong Gong: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Ruihong Yao: Investigation, Validation, Methodology. Congbin Fan: Formal analysis, Supervision. Zhao Chen: Supervision, Investigation. Shouzhi Pu: Resources, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41867053), the Science and Technology Research Project of Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ190615), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20202BAB213018).

The human serum samples were collected at the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University. All the experiments in this work were carried out in compliance with ethical standards, and conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki and approved by the Ethics Committee of Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120617.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disulfide bond-based self-crosslinked carbon-dots for turn-on fluorescence imaging of GSH in living cells
Analyst

Analyst

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-identification of glutathione based on its inhibition on the 3D ball-flower shaped Cu-hemin-MOF’s peroxidase-like activity
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, X. Wang, G. Cao, Y. Wu, H. Luo, Z. Ji, C. Shen, D. Huo, C.
